items.title: Accelerating legacy code modernization: EPAM’s journey with Amazon Q Developer
items.author: Venugopalan Vasudevan
items.content:
This post is co-written with Nazariy Popov, Volodymyr Konchuk, and Andrii Davydenko from EPAM

Legacy code modernization presents significant challenges for organizations looking to stay competitive in today’s rapidly evolving digital landscape. Organizations face the dual challenge of maintaining business continuity while modernizing their legacy systems for cloud environments. This transformation requires organizations to carefully navigate between preserving essential business logic and implementing modern architectural patterns. This is where AI-powered development tools can make a transformative impact, as demonstrated in EPAM’s recent legacy modernization project using Amazon Q Developer.

Amazon Q Developer, an AI code assistant, seamlessly integrates into the development pipeline to address these challenges. This innovative AI code assistant helps teams tackle various tasks, from generating new features, automating language upgrades, and refactoring legacy code to fixing bugs and automating deployments. By providing detailed explanations for its code suggestions while maintaining high quality standards, Amazon Q Developer significantly improves developer efficiency across the entire software development lifecycle, resulting in substantial time and effort savings.

EPAM, an AWS Premier Partner, collaborated with one of their customers to modernize their legacy applications to AWS Cloud. The modernization initiative focused on multiple business-critical applications, primarily built in Java 8 with Oracle Database backend.

In this post, you’ll learn how Amazon Q Developer helped EPAM engineers transform these complex legacy systems into modern cloud-native architectures on AWS. The tool enabled the team to autonomously perform a range of tasks—from implementing new microservices and documenting code to testing, reviewing, and refactoring Java code, as well as performing critical platform upgrades.

Before diving into the details, here’s an overview of how Amazon Q Developer helped EPAM across various aspects of the modernization project:

Summary of Amazon Q Developer Use Cases in EPAM’s Modernization Journey:

Let’s explore each of these areas in detail.

Enhancing developer efficiency (Estimated time savings: 60-70%)

Amazon Q Developer played a crucial role in boosting EPAM’s development productivity. By automating routine tasks and providing intelligent code suggestions, the tool enabled developers to focus on more strategic aspects of the modernization project. Let’s explore how EPAM leveraged these capabilities.

Use Case 1: Generating New API Endpoints

Creating new API endpoints traditionally requires developers to invest 1-2 days per endpoint, involving multiple steps from designing the API contract to writing unit tests and documentation. Using Amazon Q Developer, the team dramatically accelerated this process for three new API endpoints in an existing microservice. Q Developer efficiently generated the initial code implementation along with comprehensive unit test coverage, requiring only minor modifications such as renaming variables, enhancing error handling, and refining test cases. The unit tests generated proved remarkably reliable with minimal adjustments needed. Along with this, Q Developer also generated comprehensive comments/documentation of the code improving the maintainability. This reduced the total development time to just 4 hours for all three endpoints – a 70% time saving compared to the traditional approach, allowing developers to focus on fine-tuning business logic rather than writing boilerplate code.

Use Case 2: Integrating Legacy Systems

Integrating a legacy monolith application with modern microservices traditionally requires developers to manually write extensive integration code, taking 1-2 weeks per integration point. Amazon Q Developer accelerated this process by automatically generating REST API client code in the monolith to consume microservice endpoints, along with data transfer objects (DTOs), error handling, and retry logic with integration test templates. While developers still needed to validate business rules and fine-tune error scenarios, Q Developer’s ability to understand both the legacy monolith’s structure and modern microservice patterns reduced the integration time to 2-3 days per integration point – a 70% time saving. This significantly streamlined the integration process while maintaining the robustness required for production systems.

Use Case 3: Generating and Refactoring JPA Entity Classes

During the modernization effort, new database tables were required to support additional business functionality in both the monolith and microservices. Instead of manually coding the data access layer, Amazon Q Developer automated the process by generating Spring JPA Entity classes from SQL DDL statements. Amazon Q Developer maintained consistency with existing data models by following established naming conventions, applying standard annotations, and implementing required interfaces from the existing codebase. What stood out was Q Developer’s ability to provide detailed explanations for its implementation choices, such as why specific annotations were used or how the new entities aligned with existing persistence patterns, enabling the team to quickly validate the generated code against their architectural standards. Amazon Q Developer generated the complete Java Spring entity class with all the fields. Additionally, Amazon Q Developer refactored the Entity class as well.

Use Case 4: Streamlining Project Documentation

Creating and maintaining up-to-date project documentation is often a time-consuming task for developers. Amazon Q Developer simplified this process by assisting in the generation of README files for the team’s projects. By analyzing the project structure, dependencies, and key components, Q Developer produced initial drafts of README files that included project overviews, setup instructions, and API documentation. This allowed developers to quickly review and refine the documentation, ensuring it met team standards while saving significant time compared to writing everything from scratch.

Use Case 5: Enhancing Jira Ticket Descriptions

Writing detailed, informative Jira ticket descriptions can be a challenge, especially for complex features or bug fixes. Amazon Q Developer aided the team by suggesting detailed descriptions for Jira tickets based on the context of the code changes and related discussions. For example, when creating a ticket for a new feature, Q Developer could propose a description that included the feature’s purpose, key implementation details, and potential impact on other system components. While developers still needed to review and adjust these descriptions, the AI-generated starting point significantly reduced the time spent on ticket management, allowing the team to focus more on actual development work.

Transforming workloads (Estimated time savings: 65-75%)

Moving legacy applications to the cloud requires careful planning and execution. EPAM utilized Amazon Q Developer’s Java upgrade capabilities to streamline the transformation of monolithic applications into modern, cloud-native architectures. Here’s how the Amazon Q Developer facilitated this process.

Use Case 6: Modernizing and upgrading Java applications

Amazon Q Developer assisted in upgrading older Java applications to Java 21 to leverage modern features like Java Streams API and adapting it for Spring Boot tech stack. It not only upgraded the code, but also updated deprecated code components, dependencies and libraries as well. This modernization improved the code’s performance and also aligned it with the current best practices adopted by the development teams. For large monolithic applications, breaking/decomposing the monolith into logical groups while identifying and separating common modules as shared dependencies helped break down the problem into manageable pieces for the agent to do a better job, resulting in a more maintainable and modular structure for the transformation process. This modular approach significantly enhanced Q Developer’s ability to analyze and transform the codebase while reducing the complexity of the modernization effort.

Refactoring Code, Improving Code Quality and Readability (Estimated time savings: 60-75%)

One of the most challenging aspects of modernization is refactoring legacy code and maintaining high code quality standards. Amazon Q Developer assisted EPAM’s team in analyzing complex codebases and suggesting improvements, optimizing the code while preserving business logic and ensuring consistent code quality. The following examples demonstrate this capability in action.

Use Case 7: Refactoring Complex Methods

Legacy code often includes methods with high cyclomatic complexity, making them difficult to maintain. Amazon Q Developer helped the development team refactor large, complex methods into smaller, more readable, and better-structured methods. It also provided a detailed explanation of the changes, highlighting how the refactored code improved maintainability and readability.

Use Case 8: Renaming Across the Repository

When tasked with renaming ‘‘ to ‘‘ across the entire repository, Amazon Q Developer demonstrated capabilities beyond simple search and replace functionality found in traditional IDEs. It performed context-aware renaming, distinguishing between instances where ‘‘ was part of larger phrases or variable names, while simultaneously updating related components including unit tests, integration tests, and logging statements. The tool intelligently refactored method signatures where the report name was part of method names or parameters, analyzed and updated database queries, and maintained consistency across different file types including Java, XML, and properties files. What set Q Developer apart was its ability to provide detailed change logs explaining each modification and the rationale behind more complex refactoring decisions, significantly reducing the risk of missed references or inconsistencies that often occur with manual search-and-replace operations.

Use Case 9: Code Review and fixes

The code review capabilities of Amazon Q Developer, seamlessly integrated into the IDE, enabled the development team to detect potential issues spanning multiple classes. Beyond merely identifying problems, Q Developer provided actionable fix recommendations that could be easily reviewed and implemented. This proactive approach to code quality allowed the team to address issues during the early stages of development, significantly reducing the likelihood of defects making their way to production environments.

Diagnosing and troubleshooting errors (Estimated time savings: 40-60%)

Quick error resolution is crucial for maintaining development momentum. Amazon Q Developer’s advanced error analysis capabilities helped EPAM’s team identify and fix issues efficiently, reducing debugging time significantly. Here are some examples of how this worked in practice.

Use Case 10: Root Cause Analysis and Fix

During the development phase, the team encountered an unexpected error in one of the Java services: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException Q Developer conducted a deeper analysis based on the context provided and suggested a more targeted fix and generated the necessary Java code changes, provided unit tests to verify the fix, and outlined potential security implications of the change. This comprehensive solution not only resolved the immediate error but also improved the overall security posture of the XML processing in the application. The team was able to implement and verify the fix within minutes, significantly reducing development.

Use Case 11: Fixing Database Connection Issues

While troubleshooting an issue where the application became unresponsive due to JDBC connection problems, Amazon Q Developer analyzed the project code and identified the missing connection pool configuration. Q Developer suggested implementing essential connection pool parameters like 'maximumPoolSize=20' and 'connectionTimeout=30000' based on the application’s traffic patterns and code. After implementing its suggested configuration, the issue was resolved, significantly improving the application’s stability.

Use Case 12: Complex SQL Query Analysis

Debugging complex SQL queries constructed dynamically in Java code can be challenging. Amazon Q Developer analyzed such queries, broke them down into their component parts, and provided descriptions for query parameters. For instance, when presented with a complex query involving multiple joins and subqueries, Q Developer dissected it into logical blocks, explaining how each part contributed to the overall result set. This made it easier for the team to understand and debug the queries.

Testing and Deployment: Test data generation and Automating Infrastructure Setup (Estimated time savings: 30-50%)

Use Case 13: Generating JSON Request Bodies

When testing new APIs, Amazon Q Developer generated JSON request bodies based on the corresponding Java classes. It provided detailed descriptions of each field and suggested realistic and meaningful default values, making it easier to validate API functionality with real-world scenarios.

Use Case 14: Generating SQL Test Data

Amazon Q Developer generated SQL insert statements with test data based on our existing Java Entity classes. This automation saved us a significant amount of time in creating realistic test data for database validation and integration testing.

Use Case 15: Generating Deployment Files

Amazon Q Developer helped the development team generate essential deployment artifacts, including a Docker file, a startup shell script that was used as an entry point, and a Kubernetes deployment file for a new service. Automating this process not only saves time but also improves consistency across environments.

Ready to ransform our evelop

EPAM’s experience with Amazon Q Developer has been transformative, significantly accelerating their application modernization efforts while maintaining high code quality. By leveraging Amazon Q Developer, EPAM reduced development time by approximately 70% and improved code quality metrics across the client’s portfolio. This efficiency gains not only accelerated the client’s cloud migration timeline but also resulted in substantial cost savings and faster time-to-market for new features.

Now, it’s your turn to explore Amazon Q Developer:

Schedule a demo: Experience firsthand how Amazon Q Developer can accelerate your development lifecycle. Connect with our team for a personalized demonstration tailored to your specific use case.

Start Your Proof of Concept: Begin your journey with Amazon Q Developer today through a proof of concept. See how it can enhance your team’s productivity and code quality, just as it did for EPAM.

Connect with EPAM: Learn more about EPAM’s success story and best practices for implementing Amazon Q Developer in your organization’s development workflow.

Take the next step in revolutionizing your development process. Visit Amazon Q Developer website or contact your AWS account team to get started.

items.title: CNL: kcp – Kubernetes-Like Control Planes for Declarative APIs
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>In the Cloud Native world declarative APIs are ubiquitous, enshrined in the Kubernetes Resource Model (KRM). Kubernetes Operators are built around this concept and Platform Engineering, an emerging discipline in the ecosystem, often centers around it as well.

This continued success of the KRM raises a question: Why not use the Kubernetes API without the intention to orchestrate containers? kcp, a CNCF Sandbox project, adds stronger multi-tenancy and additional API management capabilities on top of the Kubernetes API server code. It supercharges the KRM to be used as a generic control plane for any kind of declarative APIs.

Our webinar on kcp explores the fundamental concepts of kcp, its usage of the KRM and how to publish and reconcile Kubernetes-like APIs to a multitude of users. Join us on this webinar and gain insights into unlocking all new possibilities with kcp!</article>

items.title: ChatLoopBackOff: Episode 72 (Kagent)
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]

<article>In this episode, CNCF Ambassador Satyam Soni explores Kagent, an open source observability agent designed to simplify how developers collect, process, and export telemetry data from Kubernetes environments. items.title: Introducing a Richer ”docker model run” Experience
items.author: Eric Curtin
items.content:
The command line is where developers live and breathe. A powerful and intuitive CLI can make the difference between a frustrating task and a joyful one. That’s why we’re excited to announce a major upgrade to the interactive chat experience in , our tool for running AI workloads locally.

We’ve rolled out a new, fully-featured interactive prompt for the “docker model run” command that brings a host of quality-of-life improvements, making it faster, easier, and more intuitive to chat with your local models. Let’s dive into what’s new.

A True Readline-Style Prompt with Keyboard Shortcuts

The most significant change is the move to a new readline-like implementation. If you spend any time in a modern terminal, you’ll feel right at home. This brings advanced keyboard support for navigating and editing your prompts right on the command line.

You can now use familiar keyboard shortcuts to work with your text more efficiently. Here are some of the new key bindings you can start using immediately:

  • Use “Ctrl + a” to jump to the beginning of the line and “Ctrl + e” to jump to the end.
  • Quickly move through your prompt using “Alt + b” to go back one word and “Alt + f” to go forward one word.
    • “Ctrl + k”: Delete everything from the cursor to the end of the line.
    • “Ctrl + u”: Delete everything from the cursor to the beginning of the line.
    • “Ctrl + w”: Delete the word immediately before the cursor.
  • Screen and Session Management:
    • “Ctrl + l”: Clear the terminal screen to reduce clutter.
    • “Ctrl + d”: Exit the chat session, just like the /bye command.

Take Back Control with “Ctrl + c”

We’ve all been there: you send a prompt to a model, and it starts generating a long, incorrect, or unwanted response. Previously, you had to wait for it to finish. Not anymore.

You can now press “Ctrl + c” at any time while the model is generating a response to immediately stop it. We’ve implemented this using context cancellation in our client, which sends a signal to halt the streaming response from the model. This gives you full control over the interaction, saving you time and frustration. This feature has also been added to the basic interactive mode for users who may not be in a standard terminal environment. “Ctrl + c” ends that interaction but does not exit. “Ctrl + d” exits “docker model run”.

Improved Multi-line and History Support

Working with multi-line prompts, like pasting in code snippets, is now much smoother. The prompt intelligently changes from > to a more subtle . to indicate that you’re in multi-line mode.

Furthermore, the new prompt includes command history. Simply use the Up and Down arrow keys to cycle through your previous prompts, making it easy to experiment, correct mistakes, or ask follow-up questions without retyping everything. For privacy or scripting purposes, you can disable history writing by setting the DOCKER_MODEL_NOHISTORY environment variable.

These improvements make “docker model run” a more powerful and pleasant tool for all your local AI experiments. Pull a model from Docker Hub and start a chat to experience the new prompt yourself:

$ docker model run ai/gemma3
> Tell me a joke about docker containers.
Why did the Docker container break up with the Linux host?

... Because it said, "I need some space!" 

---

Would you like to hear another one?

Help Us Build the Future of Local AI

Docker Model Runner is an open-source project, and we’re building it for the community. These updates are a direct result of our effort to create the best possible experience for developers working with AI.

We invite you to get involved!

  • and suggest new features you’d like to see.
  • with us and the community.

Your contributions help shape the future of local AI development and make powerful tools accessible to everyone. We can’t wait to see what you build!

items.title: Destroy and Deploy: the Joys of Immutability
items.author: Fernando Duran
items.content:
(Funny I chose mount Everest to illustrate immutability but the Hillary Step is no longer there)

 — Hands-On Linux & DevOps. Real Challenges. Real Infra. Real Skills.

Sysadmin Friend: “Hey, look, my application server in production has an uptime of over a thousand days! Talk about stability eh?”

Me: “Well, that’s actually not a good thing.”

Sysadmin Friend: “What!?”

Me: “Sorry, let me tell you a grandpa story.”

In the old, pre-cloud days, we would make changes in our server (upgrades, application deployments) half-manually, typically running our own custom Bash scripts in big servers named after Star Wars characters. These were our “pets” or “snowflakes” because they were hard to reproduce from scratch and we didn’t want to touch them too much, afraid they could die from a light breeze. If the sysadmin tending to the pet left the company, and as usual there was no good documentation, then, well, there would be a world of pain.

Then “the cloud” was invented and on one hand you could spin up servers quickly and easily and on the other hand they could die without notice (“what do you mean ephemeral disks?”). Also the collaboration between developers and operations (or rather doing sysadmin the developer way) was coined “DevOps” and that concept has been paying our salaries for a while.

A fundamental DevOps concept is “Infrastructure as Code”, where you declare in code how you want your infrastructure to be. Configuration Management tools like Ansible, Chef, Puppet or SaltStack are now used widely to perform changes in-place to our servers. This is a good thing but we can do better in general.

Upgrades in place can fail for any reason (e.g. a flaky network or Linux deciding to do an unattended upgrade blocking package installations) and as a result have partially upgraded servers or servers in an unknown final state. This is a problem even if it only happens a small percentage of the time (with a big fleet this will be more pronounced). We want atomic “all or nothing” operations; it adds predictability.

Instead of making changes in-place, our infrastructure can be composed of components where the whole component is replaced. We can create images of our servers (using Packer to produce AWS AMIs for example) for any change and deploy them while destroying the previous ones. Sometimes these re-created servers are called “Phoenix” servers.

Server immutability has several advantages:

  • Well-known state of servers at all times, with simple versioned history.
  • Less deployment failures.
  • Easy and fast deployments with lower downtime; simply spin up servers from the image. This also implies faster horizontal scaling out.
  • Easier consistent deployments to different environments: the same image can be used for production and testing environments. This helps a lot with testing.
  • Possibility of different deployment strategies like blue-green or canary deployments: during upgrade, incoming requests are routed from the older image version to the new one, taking advantage of the fact that there are no servers in an intermediate state.
  • Easy roll-backs to a previous version of an image.
  • No configuration drift problems in running servers.
  • Better security, since the configuration of the server is better understood and servers are more often destroyed and recreated.
  • Destroying and redeploying also uncovers issues where a reboot would fail like misconfigured initialization or some configuration living in memory only and not saved to disk. A frequent fully destroy-and-deploy procedure in test environments will find issues early.

As main drawbacks we have:

  • Need to recreate the image for any (application) change, including small ones. Note that for configuration changes or setting secrets, they can be pulled with cloud-init at spin time.
  • Full cycle of change plus deployment can be slower than applying changes unto the running server. Proper automation earlier in your CI/CD pipeline will help here.
  • Service discovery may be needed.

All this killing servers and spinning new ones is all well, but what about permanent data?

Ideally (see “The Twelve-Factor App”) the servers process stateless applications, regarding data:

  • Application logs can be shipped to a central database for storage, aggregation and search, for example using the Fluentd/Elasticsearch/Kibana or Promtail/Loki stacks.
  • Permanent data needs to be external, like a separate volume that is attached or using a database as a service from the cloud vendor.

Sysadmin Friend: “Oh I see. I guess then you like Docker and Terraform?”

Me: “Yes, but that’s for another beer night.”

items.title: Essential PromQL Queries for Knowledge Graphs & LLM Text Embeddings : Part 1
items.author: Scaibu
items.content:
Essential PromQL Queries for Knowledge Graphs & LLM Text Embeddings : Part 1

In production systems where both knowledge graphs and large-language-model embeddings power features like semantic search, reasoning, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), subtle degradations can silently erode both quality and cost-efficiency.

Traditional monitoring (CPU/GPU, latency) isn’t enough. You need domain-specific queries that surface issues like semantic drift, retrieval relevance drop, embedding model mismatch, graph structural decay, and index bloat.

Embedding Quality & Drift Detection

In AI/LLM pipelines, embeddings are a critical intermediate representation of text that power search, retrieval, and recommendation. Poor embedding quality or pipeline failures can silently degrade downstream results, leading to stale or irrelevant outputs. Continuous monitoring is essential to detect errors, inefficiencies, and drift in embeddings.

rate(embedding_generation_total{status="error"}[5m]) / rate(embedding_generation_total[5m]) * 100

This metric calculates the percentage of embedding generation attempts that fail over a rolling 5-minute window.

  • High error rates into retrieval pipelines.
  • Even a small persistent error rate (2–5%) compounds over time, leaving significant portions of your index stale.
  • Stale embeddings → , inaccurate recommendations, and .

Failures may stem from multiple sources:

  • API throttling or rate limits
  • Internal model errors that don’t always raise explicit exceptions
  • Many failures are , making detection tricky without explicit metrics.

Persistent small error rates can silently remove large amounts of data from your index. Monitoring EM6 helps catch errors before they accumulate into a major pipeline bottleneck.

  • Track over time.
  • Token length distributions

During batch PDF ingestion, the embedding error rate jumped because PDFs exceeded token limits. Truncate input text and log warnings.

2. Token Efficiency (Input/Output Ratio)

(  rate(llm_token_usage_total{token_type="input"}[5m])  rate(llm_token_usage_total{token_type="output"}[5m])

The ratio of sent to the LLM versus returned. Essentially, it measures .

  • High input/output ratios indicate inefficient use of tokens, which can increase costs and latency.
  • In retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines, excessive irrelevant context inflates this ratio without improving output quality.
  • Inefficiencies may be subtle:
  • Redundant system/context instructions
  • Unoptimized retrieval of documents
  • Ratio spikes can be hard to detect without fine-grained logging.
  • Sudden spikes indicate or retrieving excessive context.
  • Regular monitoring ensures cost-efficient and performant LLM usage.
  • Track for each model/pipeline.
  • Alert when ratio exceeds .
  • Investigate prompt or retrieval inefficiencies and optimize.

Reducing RAG pipeline context size from 7500 → 4000 tokens eliminated redundant documents → cost dropped 40% per query.

3 Embedding Batch Size Distribution

histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(embedding_batch_size_bucket[5m]))
  • Measures the 95th percentile of embedding batch sizes processed over time.
  • Helps monitor whether batch sizes are too large (causing latency spikes) or too small (underutilizing resources).
  • Batch size affects :
  • Too large → timeouts, model overload, high memory usage
  • Too small → underutilized infrastructure, slow ingestion
  • Efficient batching ensures stable pipeline performance.
  • Optimal batch size depends on:
  • Infrastructure constraints
  • Hard to tune dynamically in production environments.
  • Monitor median and P95 batch sizes.
  • Consistent batch sizes avoid sudden throughput drops or latency spikes.
  • Track median and P95 batch sizes over time.
  • If P95 exceeds threshold → adjust pipeline batch configuration.

PDF ingestion initially processed , causing latency spikes. Reducing to stabilized throughput.

Retrieval & RAG Pipeline Monitoring

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines combine document retrieval with LLM generation to provide context-aware responses. Monitoring these pipelines ensures low latency, cost efficiency, and high relevance. Metrics must capture not just raw speed but also quality and efficiency of retrieval.

Query RAG 1 — Hybrid Retrieval Latency (Vector + Graph)

histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(rag_retrieval_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
by (le, retrieval_method))
  • The 95th percentile latency of the , split by method (vector, graph, or hybrid).
  • Captures extreme latency events without being skewed by outliers.
  • High retrieval latency directly degrades user experience in RAG systems.
  • Latency spikes can affect real-time applications and may trigger SLA violations.

Hybrid retrieval pipelines involve :

  • Knowledge-graph traversal
  • Bottlenecks can depending on query content, dataset size, or infrastructure load.
  • at normal load signals inefficient retrieval or index imbalance.
  • Monitoring P95 provides an early warning before users experience slow responses.
  • Track vector-only vs graph-only vs hybrid retrieval separately.
  • Tune retrieval strategies or caching based on observed latency patterns.
  • Hybrid retrieval P95 = 5s → switched to vector-only with cached entities → latency reduced to 2.5s, no quality loss.

Query RAG 2 — Number of Documents Retrieved P95

histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(rag_documents_retrieved_bucket[5m]))
  • The 95th percentile of the number of documents retrieved per query.
  • Detects excessive retrieval that may be invisible in averages.
  • Excessive retrieval , increasing .
  • Unexpected increases in retrieved documents indicate filtering or top-k selection issues.
  • Document selection logic can change dynamically:
  • Data anomalies may silently increase retrieval sets, inflating cost and latency without obvious errors.
  • P95 > expected top_k → indicates inefficiency or a pipeline bug; corrective action is required.
  • Plot top_k trends and correlate with LLM token consumption.
  • Alert when retrieval exceeds expected bounds.
  • Retrieval returned 20 documents instead of 5 due to a filter bug → doubled API cost.

Query RAG 3 — RAG End-to-End Duration

histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(rag_end_to_end_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
  • End-to-end latency of the RAG pipeline including:
  • Directly affects .
  • Long delays reduce perceived responsiveness and user satisfaction.

End-to-end duration depends on :

  • Retrieval and embedding pipelines
  • Root cause analysis requires correlating latency with specific pipeline stages.
  • Spikes in P95 often .
  • Correlation with stage-level metrics helps isolate bottlenecks.
  • Monitor end-to-end duration; alert if duration exceeds SLA (e.g., 3s).
  • Use stage-level telemetry for debugging.
  • Vector dimension increase caused P95 latency jump to 6s → re-optimized vector search index → reduced to 2.5s.

Query RAG 4 — Context Token Size P95

histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(rag_context_size_tokens_bucket[5m]))
  • Size of the context passed to the LLM in tokens (95th percentile).
  • Large context risks , causing:

Context size is dynamic, depending on:

  • Number of top-k documents
  • Hard to predict and control in production.
  • Monitor P95 relative to model token limit.
  • Reduce top_k or chunk size proactively to avoid truncation.
  • Track context token size trends.
  • Adjust retrieval dynamically if close to model limit
  • P95 context = 7500 tokens → reduced to 4000 → hallucinations eliminated, cost dropped 35%.

Query RAG 5 — Retrieval Relevance Score

histogram_quantile(0.50, rate(rag_relevance_score_bucket[5m]))
  • Median semantic relevance of retrieved documents.
  • Captures whether retrieval results remain meaningful to downstream LLM queries.
  • Declining relevance → signals:
  • Retrieval logic regressions
  • Poor relevance impacts answer quality, even if latency is low.
  • Relevance is and may drift slowly.
  • Often only noticeable through QA or user feedback.
  • Median score drop ≥10% → trigger investigation.
  • Evaluate embeddings, retrieval filters, or graph integrity.
  • Track median relevance over time.
  • Alert if the median falls below a defined threshold.
  • Median score dropped from 0.8 → 0.5 after new dataset ingestion → re-embedded using consistent model → restored relevance.

Ingestion & Pipeline Health Monitoring

A robust ingestion pipeline is critical to keep your RAG system fresh, consistent, and performant. Metrics in this category ensure that document queues are managed, processing is efficient, and chunk sizes are optimal. Monitoring these prevents stale embeddings, latency spikes, and cost overruns.

Query ING 1 — Document Queue Backlog

  • The current number of documents waiting in the ingestion queue.
  • Indicates pipeline saturation or lag in processing.
  • High backlog → delayed embeddings → stale retrieval results.
  • Directly affects real-time or near-real-time ingestion pipelines.
  • Queue growth can happen silently while upstream stages continue to feed data.
  • Backlog might accumulate without triggering obvious errors.
  • Persistent queue size above threshold signals need for or investigating bottlenecks.
  • Set alerts if queue exceeds a threshold (e.g., 5,000 documents).
  • Monitor trends for spikes or recurring backlog patterns.
  • Overnight batch PDF ingestion built a 5k document backlog → added 10 workers → cleared in 30 minutes.

Query ING 2 — Document Processing Rate

sum(rate(document_processing_total[5m])) by (document_type)
  • Throughput of documents processed per type over a 5-minute window.
  • Helps identify which document types might be slowing down the pipeline.
  • Ensures ingestion keeps up with .
  • Slow processing → downstream embedding staleness and retrieval delays.
  • Overall rate depends on multiple pipeline stages:
  • A bottleneck may occur at and varies by document type.
  • A drop in processing rate requires a stage-level investigation to isolate slow components.
  • Alert if throughput falls below baseline or expected capacity
  • PDF processing is slower than plain text → added GPU acceleration to the embedding stage → throughput improved.

Query ING 3 — Document Processing Latency by Stage

histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(document_processing_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, stage))
  • P95 latency per stage in the ingestion pipeline.
  • Captures extreme delays and identifies slowest stages.
  • Helps for targeted optimization.
  • Long latency in one stage can block the entire pipeline.
  • Multiple stages with heterogeneous document sizes and batch processing.
  • Variability in latency can be caused by batch size, document type, or external system delays
  • P95 latency spikes at a particular stage (e.g., “embed”) → immediate investigation required.
  • Track per stage; alert if P95 exceeds stage SLA.
  • Use to optimize batch size, parallelism, or model selection.
  • Embed stage P95 latency = 2s → reduced batch size → latency dropped to 0.5s → balanced throughput across stages.

Query ING 4 — Document Chunk Size Distribution

histogram_quantile(0.50, rate(document_chunk_size_bytes_bucket[5m])) / 1024
  • Median document chunk size (in KB) processed by the pipeline.
  • Ensures chunks fit within efficiently.
  • Large chunks → exceed LLM context, cause truncation, hallucinations, and higher cost.
  • Small chunks → increase overhead, more embeddings, and unnecessary computation.
  • Chunk size varies due to:
  • Maintaining consistency requires active monitoring.
  • Median chunk size above threshold → consider splitting text differently or adjusting chunking logic.
  • Monitor median chunk size trends.
  • Alert if median exceeds safe limit for target LLM context window.
  • PDF chunk median = 5 KB → reduced to 1 KB → context fits 8k token window efficiently.

Vector Database (Qdrant) Monitoring

Vector databases like Qdrant are the backbone of embedding-based retrieval pipelines. Continuous monitoring ensures high performance, stability, and search relevance. Metrics focus on search throughput, latency, ingestion, memory usage, embedding quality, and error rates.

Query VEC 1 — Qdrant Search Rate

sum(rate(qdrant_search_total[5m])) by (collection)
  • Number of vector search operations per second per collection.
  • Reflects on each collection.
  • Ensures collections can .
  • Sudden spikes may indicate unusual query patterns or bot activity, potentially overloading the system.
  • Different collections have different baseline traffic.
  • High load may trigger or slow responses.
  • Monitor for , which may require resource scaling or traffic investigation.
  • Plot search rate per collection.
  • Alert when the rate exceeds the expected baseline.
  • “Documents” collection query rate jumped from 50 → 200/sec during batch query → scaled resources to handle load.

Query VEC 2 — Qdrant P95 Search Latency

histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(qdrant_search_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
  • 95th percentile latency of searches per collection.
  • Captures that impact end-user experience.
  • High latency directly affects in search and retrieval.
  • Ensures SLA adherence for real-time systems.
  • Index type (HNSW, IVF, etc.)
  • Identifying the cause requires correlating latency with and .
  • P95 latency exceeding SLA → signals need for index optimisation, quantisation, or infrastructure scaling.
  • Track trends to identify early performance degradation.
  • P95 latency = 800ms → applied HNSW indexing → reduced to 150ms.

Query VEC 3 — Qdrant Upsert Rate

rate(qdrant_upsert_total[5m])
  • Vector insertion throughput (points per second).
  • Indicates ingestion pipeline performance.
  • Ensures the database can keep up with incoming embeddings.
  • Smooth upsert rate prevents pipeline backpressure.
  • Burst insertions can or cause .
  • Requires careful balancing of batch size, concurrency, and system resources.
  • Maintain stable throughput to avoid or .
  • Correlate spikes with batch size, number of points, or ingestion pipeline events.
  • Upsert rate = 10/sec → batched to 200/sec → ingestion stabilized.

Query VEC 4 — Qdrant Memory Usage

qdrant_collection_memory_bytes / 1e9
  • Memory usage per collection in GB.
  • Reflects collection size, vector dimension, and index type.
  • Prevents out-of-memory (OOM) crashes.
  • Memory growth correlates with collection expansion and vector size.
  • Memory usage is , influenced by:
  • Index type (HNSW, scalar, quantized)
  • Unexpected growth can occur if not monitored.
  • Threshold alerts enable timely quantization or pruning to reduce memory footprint.
  • Monitor memory per collection.
  • Enable quantization or prune old vectors when approaching limit.
  • 50 GB memory usage → applied scalar quantization → reduced to 12 GB.

Query VEC 5 — Qdrant Search Score Distribution

histogram_quantile(0.50, rate(qdrant_search_score_bucket[5m]))
  • Median similarity score of search results per collection.
  • Indicates and .
  • Declining scores → signals embedding drift, model changes, or index misalignment.
  • Direct impact on .
  • Score drift can be .
  • New embeddings may degrade quality subtly, requiring continuous monitoring.
  • Low median score → investigate embeddings, retraining, or index alignment.
  • Alert if median similarity < 0.6.
  • Score dropped from 0.85 → 0.6 → updated embedding model restored relevance.

Query VEC 6 — Qdrant Error Rate

sum(rate(qdrant_error_total[5m]))/ (sum(rate(qdrant_search_total[5m])) 
+ sum(rate(qdrant_upsert_total[5m]))) 
* 100
  • Percentage of failed vector operations (search or upsert) per collection.
  • Detects , critical for pipeline reliability.
  • Unnoticed errors can cause or incomplete retrieval.
  • Errors may be intermittent.
    • Persistent error rate >2% → immediate investigation required.
    • Alert on threshold breaches to ensure .
    • Backup process caused 5% errors → adjusted schedule → issue resolved.

    LLM Usage & Cost Monitoring

    Monitoring LLM usage ensures cost efficiency, reliable performance, and SLA adherence. Metrics focus on request volume, latency, error rates, token consumption, context usage, retries, concurrency, and costs. These provide visibility into both and of LLM-powered applications.

    Query LLM 1 — Request Rate

    rate(llm_request_total[5m])
    • Number of LLM requests per second over a rolling 5-minute window.
    • Reflects API usage volume and pipeline load.
    • Tracks overall .
    • Helps with and early detection of abnormal spikes that could overwhelm rate limits.
    • LLM usage is ; sudden traffic spikes can cause throttling or failed requests.
    • Baselines vary across models and time windows.
    • Continuous monitoring prevents rate-limit errors (HTTP 429) and ensures smooth service.
    • Plot request rate over time.
    • Alert if requests exceed .
    • Traffic spike detected → throttled non-critical queries → avoided 429 errors.
    sum(rate(llm_request_total{status="error"}[5m]))/ sum(rate(llm_request_total[5m])) * 100
    • Percentage of LLM requests that failed (per model or overall).
    • Early warning for API or integration issues.
    • Sustained errors can impact SLAs and user experience.
    • Errors may be transient or intermittent.
    • Requires over time to identify persistent issues.
    • Even 1–2% sustained errors can .
    • Investigate patterns by request type, model, or prompt size.
    • Errors concentrated on GPT-4 → API quota exceeded → scaled quota → resolved.

    Query LLM 3 — P95 Latency

    histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(llm_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
    • 95th percentile of request latency per model.
    • Captures , critical for real-world user experience.
    • Average latency may hide that degrade user experience.
    • Ensures SLA compliance for response times.
    • Prompt size and complexity
    • P95 > SLA → urgent optimization required (e.g., reduce context size or batch requests).
    • Correlate spikes with prompt size, context window, or RAG retrieval.
    • GPT-4 P95 latency = 4s → context reduced → latency dropped to 2s.

    Query LLM 4 — Cost Per Hour

    rate(llm_cost_dollars_total[1h]) * 3600
    • Estimated on LLM usage.
    • Prevents unexpected .
    • Helps in financial monitoring and planning.
    • Costs fluctuate dynamically based on:
    • Sudden spikes often indicate runaway prompts or misconfigured pipelines.
    • Alert if hourly spend exceeds budget thresholds.
    • Investigate unexpected spikes immediately.
    • Unexpected cost spike → identified redundant prompt duplication → fixed → normalized spend.

    Query LLM 5 — Token Consumption Rate

    rate(llm_token_usage_total[1m]) * 60
    • Tokens consumed per minute (input + output).
    • Reflects both and .
    • Avoids hitting .
    • Helps optimize and RAG pipeline usage.
    • Token usage fluctuates with:
    • Number of retrieved documents
    • High token rate → consider throttling low-priority queries.
    • Monitor and alert when usage exceeds threshold.
    • Correlate with top-k retrieval changes or prompt length.
    • Top-k retrieval increased from 5 → 20 docs → token usage tripled → adjusted back to 5 → normalized.

    Query LLM 6 — Context Window Utilization

    histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(llm_context_window_usage_ratio_bucket[5m])) * 100
    • 95th percentile of context window usage (%) relative to model limits.
    • Prevents exceeding LLM context window → avoids truncation and hallucinations.
    • Ensures stable and accurate responses.
    • Dynamic prompt sizes and RAG retrieval context cause .
    • P95 usage > 90% → reduce context size, number of retrieved documents, or chunk size.
    • Track top-k, chunk size, and prompt length.
    • Adjust dynamically to maintain safe context utilization.
    • Context usage = 95% → trimmed documents → improved accuracy.
    rate(llm_retry_total[5m]) / rate(llm_request_total[5m]) * 100
    • Percentage of requests retried due to failures.
    • Indicates API instability or network issues.
    • Persistent retries can affect SLA and user experience.
    • Early detection prevents cascading failures.
    • Transient failures can .
    • Requires trend analysis over time.
    • Sustained retry rate >5% → investigate immediately.
    • Alert on threshold breaches.
    • Correlate with model type or network incidents.
    • GPT-3 retries spiked → network misconfiguration → resolved → retries normalised.

    Query LLM 8 — Concurrent Requests

    • Number of LLM requests in-flight at any moment.
    • Reflects on LLM services.
    • Prevents exceeding → avoids throttling or queue overflow.
    • Dynamic traffic spikes can occur unpredictably.
    • Requires active throttling or load balancing.
    • Continuous monitoring ensures and SLA compliance.
    • Throttle low-priority queries if concurrent requests exceed safe thresholds.
    • Track peak concurrency to plan resource scaling.
    • 200 concurrent requests exceeded safe limit → queued low-priority queries → prevented failures.

    Embedding Generation Metrics

    Embedding generation is a critical step in ingestion pipelines for retrieval-augmented systems. Monitoring these metrics ensures high throughput, low latency, minimal errors, and cost-effective processing. These metrics provide visibility into pipeline health, batching efficiency, and resource usage.

    Query EMB 1 — Embedding Generation Rate

    rate(embedding_generation_total[5m])
    • Number of embeddings generated per second over a 5-minute window.
    • Indicates and overall processing capacity.
    • Low embedding rate → ingestion pipeline bottleneck → delays downstream RAG results.
    • Ensures in real-time or batch pipelines.

    Embedding generation is .

      • Sustained low rate may indicate:
      • LLM API throttling or latency
      • Alert if rate drops below threshold.
      • Correlate with batch size and document type.
      • Processing 5 docs/sec → optimized batching → throughput increased to 50 docs/sec.

      Query EMB 2 — Embedding P95 Latency

      histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(embedding_generation_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
      • 95th percentile latency for embedding generation per model.
      • Captures that can impact throughput.
      • High latency reduces overall throughput → increases .
      • SLA-sensitive pipelines require monitoring P95, not just averages.
      • Latency spikes may occur due to:
      • Network or infrastructure issues
      • High P95 latency → reduces embedding throughput and may stall ingestion.
      • Monitor latency per batch size and model.
      • Optimize batch size or parallelism to achieve fastest throughput.
      • Batch of 50 texts took 500ms → optimized batch size → throughput improved 10×.

      Query EMB 3 — Embedding Error Rate

      rate(embedding_generation_total{status="error"}[5m]) / rate(embedding_generation_total[5m])
      • Percentage of failed embedding generation attempts.
      • Tracks and data coverage.
      • Errors reduce coverage in embedding index, degrading RAG results.
      • Even 5–10% sustained errors can significantly affect downstream quality.
      • Failures may be caused by:
      • Excessively long documents
      • Persistent errors → investigate input documents and API status.
      • Alert when error rate exceeds the threshold
      • Inspect failed documents and implement corrective measures (truncation, retries).
      • Texts >8192 tokens caused 20% errors → added truncation → errors resolved.

      Query EMB 4 — Embedding Batch Size

      histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(embedding_batch_size_bucket[5m]))
      • 95th percentile of embedding batch sizes over time.
      • Reflects typical and extreme batch sizes in production.
      • Batch size directly impacts:
      • Latency (too large → timeouts)
      • Throughput (too small → underutilized resources)
      • Optimal batch size depends on:
      • Large batches → potential timeout or latency spikes.
      • Small batches → inefficient utilization of resources.
      • Adjust batch size dynamically for consistent performance.
      • Monitor trends to prevent bottlenecks.
      • Batch size 100 → timeouts occurred → reduced to 50 → throughput stabilized.

      Query EMB 5 — Embedding Token Usage

      histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(embedding_token_count_bucket[5m]))
      • 95th percentile of tokens processed per embedding request.
      • Indicates resource utilization and cost per embedding.
      • High token usage → increases latency, memory consumption, and cost.
      • Helps plan infrastructure and optimize chunking strategies.
      • Token count varies due to:
      • Document length variations
      • High token count → may require or optimizing embeddings.
      • Monitor token distribution per batch.
      • Adjust chunking or preprocessing to reduce cost and improve throughput.
      • Average 6k tokens per text → optimized chunking → improved cost efficiency and throughput.

      Conclusion: Mastering Observability for Knowledge Graphs, LLMs, and Embeddings

      In modern AI-driven applications, especially those combining knowledge graphs, large language models, and embeddings, observability is not optional — it’s mission-critical. Monitoring raw metrics alone isn’t enough; understanding why a metric matters, where bottlenecks occur, and how to react separates robust, scalable systems from fragile ones.

      This guide has provided a comprehensive suite of PromQL queries, covering:

      • request rates, error rates, latency, concurrency, token usage, and cost projections.
      • generation rates, batch sizes, memory usage, error rates, and queue backlogs.

      Each query exposes a potential failure point — whether it’s a hidden memory spike, an overburdened embedding pipeline, a slow graph traversal, or a cost explosion from LLM usage. Without proactive monitoring, these issues often appear only when they affect users, leading to downtime, slow responses, or runaway costs.

      • Developers can track queue lengths and backlogs to scale workers dynamically.
      • Operations teams can detect cost anomalies and SLA violations before they impact users.
      • Data engineers can identify inefficient queries or oversized embeddings and optimize batch sizes or indexing strategies.

      Observability is a , not just a monitoring checkbox. By using these , you gain real-time insights into system health, performance, and efficiency, enabling you to maintain reliability, optimize costs, and ensure quality responses from AI pipelines.

      With this cheat-sheet, teams now have a ready-to-use, production-focused observability framework for LLMs, embeddings, knowledge graphs, and RAG pipelines — a toolkit that saves time, prevents failures, and powers high-performing AI applications.
