items.title: Your First Kubernetes App: Running MongoDB and Mongo Express Locally with Minikube
items.author: Shahul H
items.content:
When I first heard people talk about Kubernetes, it sounded abstract with their pods, services, configmaps, deployments, secrets, namespace and others it felt like learning a new language. So I decided to learn it the only way I know how is, by building something real.

So, in my previous article, when I first spun up my Minikube cluster, it was completely empty, a blank canvas. No pods, no deployments, just a single default service staring back at me.

My goal was simple but exciting: to deploy and , two simple apps that perfectly demonstrate how a frontend and backend connect inside Kubernetes.

I chose these two because they perfectly illustrate a very common setup in real-world projects a that talks to a . Once you understand this, you can easily apply the same pattern to many other apps you’ll deploy in the future.

1. Understanding the Setup

We want to run , which will serve as our database, and , which is a simple web-based admin interface for MongoDB.

But to make these two talk to each other properly inside a Kubernetes cluster, we need to set up a few components.

First, we’ll create a , and to allow other components within the cluster to communicate with it, we’ll create an .

Now, internal means that only components inside the cluster can access it no outside traffic can reach MongoDB directly. That’s exactly what we want for a database.

Then, we’ll deploy , our front-end web app. Mongo Express will need two things to connect to MongoDB:

  1. The so it knows where MongoDB is.
  2. The a username and a password to authenticate the connection.

We’ll pass both pieces of information to Mongo Express as in its deployment configuration.

To keep things organised and secure, we’ll store the database URL in a and the credentials in a and we’ll reference them from inside the Mongo Express deployment.

Finally, because Mongo Express is a web app we want to access from our browser, we’ll expose it through an , which allows external requests into the cluster.

Once we set everything up, the request flow will look like this:

  • A request comes from your to the of Mongo Express.
  • That service forwards the request to the .
  • Mongo Express then connects through the of MongoDB.
  • The internal Service sends the request to the , where the credentials are verified.
  • The data flows back through the same path, and you see the result in your browser.

That’s the big picture.Now, let’s build it step-by-step.

The first thing we’ll create is the . Let’s call it mongo.yaml

apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment  name: mongodb-deployment    app: mongodb  replicas: 1    matchLabels:  template:       labels:    spec:      - name: mongodb        ports:        env:
        - name: MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME
          valueFrom:              name: mongodb-secret        - name: MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD            secretKeyRef:              key: mongo-root-password apiVersion: v1metadata:spec:    app: mongodb  # must match the label of the pod  - protocol: TCP    targetPort: 27017   # must match the containerPort of the pod
  • We’re creating one replica of the MongoDB Pod.
  • The container uses the mongo:latest image from Docker Hub.
  • It listens on port , which is MongoDB’s default port.
  • The Service named mongodb-service exposes the MongoDB Pod on port 27017.
  • By default, this Service type (ClusterIP) exposes MongoDB within the Kubernetes cluster.
  • The type: ClusterIP isn’t specified here because it’s the default service type in Kubernetes. It ensures that the database can’t be accessed from outside the cluster which is exactly what we want for an internal database.
  • Finally, the credentials are not written directly in this file they’ll come from a we’ll create next. Those values are passed to the container through environment variables at startup MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME and MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_PASSWORD.

This approach keeps our configuration secure. You never want plain-text passwords inside files that might be committed to version control.

Now, let’s define the that stores our admin username and password.Here’s what the file mongo-secret.yaml looks like:

apiVersion: v1kind: Secret  name: mongodb-secretdata:
  mongo-root-username: dXNlcm5hbWU=     #base64 encoded
  mongo-root-password: cGFzc3dvcmQ=     #base64 encoded

Each value here is , not plain text.You can generate them by running this command in your terminal:

echo -n 'username' | base64echo -n 'password' | base64

Then copy those encoded strings into your file. Now we’ll apply the secret before the deployment because the deployment references it.

If the Secret doesn’t exist yet, Kubernetes will throw an error.

So, we’ll apply the Secret first, followed by the MongoDB Deployment:

kubectl apply -f mongo-secret.yamlkubectl apply -f mongo.yaml

After a few seconds, the MongoDB Pod will start. You can check its status with:

kubectl get podkubectl get all

If you want to confirm that MongoDB is running, you can describe the Pod or check its logs.

5. Creating the ConfigMap

When MongoDB was up and running, I moved on to Mongo Express. It needed two things to connect to which is MongoDB Address/ Internal Service, and Credentials to Authenticate

But before we do that, we’ll need a to store the MongoDB URL.Let’s call it mongo-configmap.yaml

apiVersion: v1kind: ConfigMap  name: mongodb-configmap  database_url: mongodb-service
Notice that the value of database_url matches the of MongoDB in mongo.yamlthat’s how Mongo Express will find it within the cluster.

By keeping this information in a ConfigMap instead of hardcoding it into our deployment file, we make our configuration more flexible and easier to maintain. If the Service name ever changes, we can simply update the ConfigMap without touching the deployment itself.

5. Deploying Mongo Express

Next, we define the . Let’s call it mongo-express.yaml

apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: mongo-express    app: mongo-express  replicas: 1    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:       - name: mongo-express        ports:        env:
        - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINUSERNAME
          valueFrom:               name: mongodb-secret        - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_ADMINPASSWORD            secretKeyRef:              key: mongo-root-password
        - name: ME_CONFIG_MONGODB_SERVER
          valueFrom:              name: mongodb-configmap---kind: Service  name: mongo-express-service  selector:  type: LoadBalancer  - protocol: TCP    targetPort: 8081
  • We’re running one replica of the mongo-express container, listening on port that’s the default web interface port for Mongo Express.
  • The Service type is set toLoadBalancer, which makes Mongo Express accessible from outside the cluster. In Minikube, this doesn't create a real external load balancer like in cloud providers.
  • We explicitly set nodePort: 30000 to make the port predictable during local testing. This is optional if removed, Minikube will assign a random port instead.

The environment variables here are the key part:

  • The admin username and password are fetched securely from the same we used for MongoDB.
  • The MongoDB server address comes from the we just created.

This shows how Kubernetes resources work together:

  1. Secrets for sensitive data
  2. ConfigMaps for configuration
  3. Deployments for container management

Now let’s apply these YAML files to create the resources in our cluster:

If the ConfigMap doesn’t exist yet, Kubernetes will throw an error because the Deployment depends on it.

So, we’ll apply the ConfigMap first, followed by the Mongo Express Deployment:

kubectl apply -f mongo-configmap.yaml kubectl apply -f mongo-express.yaml

You can check its status with:

kubectl get servicekubectl get pod

6. Accessing Mongo Express Webpage

In “normal” Kubernetes clusters the LoadBalancer service would get an external IP address. Because we’re in a minikube cluster, the external IP address just shows <pending>.

To assign an IP address accessible by our local browser we execute:

minikube service mongo-express-service

which assigns an IP address and opens the browser pointing to this IP address and the external port of the specified service (or in the case of minikube running in a docker container, it opens a tunnel into the docker container)

You can access the web page via the URL

With this setup, we now have:

  • A MongoDB database running internally (secured and isolated)
  • A Mongo Express frontend accessible through a browser, communicating with MongoDB inside the cluster.

That moment was different.It wasn’t just about seeing the dashboard load it was about realizing each Kubernetes component existed.

  • : for plain-text config like URLs
  • : for sensitive credentials
  • : for running app containers
  • : for connecting everything together

Each part had a role, and when they came together, it felt like seeing the system breathe for the first time.

Kubernetes wasn’t abstract anymore it was logical, structured, and alive.

items.title: Uptrace v2.0: How ClickHouse JSON Type Accelerates Trace Queries by 10x
items.author: Uptrace
items.content:
Uptrace v2.0 introduces native ClickHouse JSON type support for storing observability data, resulting in 10x query performance improvements. This guide covers deployment, configuration, and optimization strategies for production environments.

Key improvements in v2.0:

  • Native JSON column storage with dot notation queries
  • UI-based data transformations
  • Flexible retention policies per data type
  • Built-in Let’s Encrypt SSL automation
  • Enhanced query builder with autocomplete

The Problem with Traditional Approaches

Observability systems handle nested JSON structures with unpredictable attribute sets. Each span or log entry contains variable fields:

{  "trace_id": "abc123",
  "service_name": "checkout",
  "http.method": "POST",
  "http.target": "/api/v1/orders/12345",
  "user_id": "user_987",  "db.statement": "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id = ?"

Traditional storage approaches have significant limitations:

Approach 1: Flattened Schema

CREATE TABLE spans (  trace_id String,  http_method String,  user_id String,
  -- Hundreds of columns for all possible attributes
)
  • ETL pipeline required for each new attribute

Approach 2: String Storage + JSONExtractString

CREATE TABLE spans (  trace_id String,
  attributes String  -- Raw JSON string
)
-- Query performance issue:SELECT count()WHERE JSONExtractString(attributes, 'service_name') = 'checkout';
  • JSON parsing on every query
  • No indexing on nested fields
  • 2.7 seconds on 50M records

What Changed in Uptrace v2.0

ClickHouse introduced a native JSON data type, and Uptrace v2.0 fully leverages this capability.

SELECT count()FROM spans_old
WHERE JSONExtractString(attributes, 'service_name') = 'checkout';
-- Result: 2.754 seconds on 50M records-- Processed: 50.00 million rows, 8.43 GB
SELECT count()FROM spans_new
WHERE attributes.service_name = 'checkout';
-- Result: 0.287 seconds on 50M records ⚡-- Processed: 50.00 million rows, 3.12 GB

10x performance improvement achieved through:

  1. JSON parsing during insertion (once)
  2. Columnar storage of JSON data
  3. Native indexing on nested fields
  4. Direct dot notation access
  • Docker and Docker Compose
  • 4GB RAM minimum (8GB recommended)
# Clone repositorygit clone https://github.com/uptrace/uptrace
cd uptrace/example/docker
# Start all servicesdocker-compose up -d
  •  — Metadata storage
  • Username: admin@uptrace.local

The default setup includes:

services:  clickhouse:    # Data storage engine
  postgres:      # Metadata and configuration
  uptrace:       # Main application
  otelcol:       # OpenTelemetry Collector
  redis:         # Caching layer

Navigate to Organization → New Org → New Project to create your first project through the web interface.

  • Project identifier (e.g., production)
  • Company/team name
  • Auto-generated for OTLP authentication

Configuration File Alternative

For infrastructure-as-code deployments, use uptrace.yml:

seed_data:  users:      name: Admin      password: change_this_password    - key: main_org    - key: org_admin      user: admin_user    - key: prod_project      org: main_org

The key field enables declarative resource management. Uptrace automatically creates, updates, or removes resources based on configuration changes.

Sending Traces with OpenTelemetry

Example Node.js integration:

const { NodeTracerProvider } = require('@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-node');const { BatchSpanProcessor } = require('@opentelemetry/sdk-trace-base');
const { OTLPTraceExporter } = require('@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-http');
const { Resource } = require('@opentelemetry/resources');
const { SemanticResourceAttributes } = require('@opentelemetry/semantic-conventions');
// Configure exporterconst exporter = new OTLPTraceExporter({
  url: 'http://localhost:14318/v1/traces',
  headers: {
    'uptrace-dsn': 'http://project_token@localhost:14318/1'
  }
// Setup providerconst provider = new NodeTracerProvider({    [SemanticResourceAttributes.SERVICE_NAME]: 'api-service',
    [SemanticResourceAttributes.SERVICE_VERSION]: '1.0.0',
  })
provider.addSpanProcessor(new BatchSpanProcessor(exporter));provider.register();
// Use in applicationconst tracer = provider.getTracer('api-service');
async function handleRequest(req, res) {  const span = tracer.startSpan('handleRequest', {      'http.method': req.method,      'user_id': req.user?.id  });
  try {    await processOrder(req.body);
    span.setStatus({ code: 1 }); // OK
  } catch (error) {
    span.recordException(error);
    span.setStatus({ code: 2, message: error.message }); // ERROR  } finally {  }

Uptrace v2.0 introduces an enhanced query builder with several powerful features:

1. Attribute Autocomplete

The query builder provides intelligent suggestions for available attributes as you type. Start typing user_ and see all user-related attributes.

Temporarily disable query conditions without deleting them, useful for debugging and exploration.

Combine structured filters with full-text search:

where service_name = "checkout" | search error|timeout|failed

This query finds all spans from the checkout service containing the words "error", "timeout", or "failed" in any attribute or log message.

Example: Debugging Failed Payment

where attributes.user_id = "user_12345"   and timestamp >= now() - interval 1 hour
  | search payment|stripe|checkout

This query locates all payment-related spans for a specific user within the last hour.

Data transformations process telemetry data before storage, enabling:

    Use Case 1: Reducing URL Cardinality

    Dynamic URLs create high cardinality:

    /user/123/orders/456/user/124/orders/457...

    This causes index bloat and slow queries.

    // Project → Transformations → New OperationsetAttr("http_target",     attr("http_target"),     "/user/{userId}/orders/{orderId}");

    All URLs normalized to:

    /user/{userId}/orders/{orderId}

    Use Case 2: Type Conversion

    Some libraries send numeric values as strings:

    {  "elapsed_ms": "1234.56"  // String instead of number
    setAttr("elapsed_ms", parseFloat(attr("elapsed_ms")))

    Enable aggregate functions:

    SELECT avg(attributes.elapsed_ms) FROM spans;

    Remove personally identifiable information for GDPR compliance:

    if (has(attr("user.email"))) {  setAttr("user.email", "***@***")

    Uptrace uses expr-lang for transformation logic, providing a simple yet powerful expression language.

    Project → Transformations → New Operation

    Different data types require different retention periods:

    • Recent debugging (7 days)
    • Audit trail (30 days)
    • Long-term trends (90 days)
    projects:  - name: production      spans: 168h      # 7 days
          logs: 720h       # 30 days  
          events: 720h     # 30 days
          metrics: 2160h   # 90 days

    Settings → Project → Data Retention

    • Retention all types for 90 days: 9TB
    • Retention with 7/30/90 split: 4TB

    For zero-downtime migration from v1.x or other systems, use parallel deployment:

    ┌─────────────┐│   Services  │       │       ││ OTel Collector       │   │                │┌──▼────┐      ┌───▼─────┐
    │ v1.x  │      │ v2.0    │
    │ (old) │      │ (new)   │
    └───────┘      └─────────┘

    OpenTelemetry Collector Configuration

    exporters:  otlphttp/v1:
        endpoint: http://uptrace-v1:14318
        headers:
          uptrace-dsn: "http://token_v1@uptrace-v1:14318/1"
        endpoint: http://uptrace-v2:14318      uptrace-dsn: "http://token_v2@uptrace-v2:14318/1"
    service:  pipelines:      receivers: [otlp]      exporters: [otlphttp/v1, otlphttp/v2]  # Dual export
    1. Deploy v2.0 alongside existing v1.x
    2. Configure dual export to both instances
    3. Monitor for 7 days to ensure stability
    4. Migrate dashboards and alerts to v2.0

    Real-world performance comparison on 500M spans over 7 days:

    Query 1: Top 5 Slowest Endpoints

    SELECT   attributes.http_target as endpoint,  quantile(0.95)(duration) as p95_durationWHERE service_name = 'api-gateway'
      AND timestamp >= now() - interval 24 hour
    GROUP BY endpoint
    ORDER BY p95_duration DESC
    LIMIT 5;

      Query 2: User Error Traces

      SELECT   trace_id,  attributes.error_messageWHERE attributes.user_id = 'user_12345'
        AND attributes.error = true
        AND timestamp >= now() - interval 7 day
        1. Native indexing on nested fields
        2. Zero JSON parsing overhead per query

        Uptrace v2.0 includes built-in Let’s Encrypt integration:

        # uptrace.ymlcertmagic:  staging_ca: false  # Use true for testing
        listen:  https:    domain: uptrace.example.com
        • Auto-renewal every 60 days
        • Domain DNS points to server
        • Port 80 open for HTTP-01 challenge

        ✅ Microservices architecture (5+ services) ✅ High query performance requirements

        ✅ Need unified traces, logs, and metrics

        ✅ Comfortable running ClickHouse

        ✅ Want flexible data transformations

        ❌ Small projects (1–2 services) ❌ Existing Grafana stack working well ❌ No infrastructure management capability

        Uptrace v2.0’s adoption of ClickHouse JSON type provides substantial performance improvements for observability workloads. The 10x query acceleration comes from architectural changes that eliminate JSON parsing overhead while maintaining storage flexibility.

        • Native JSON storage with columnar performance
        • Real-time data transformations
        • Flexible retention policies
        • Unified observability platform

items.title: High-Concurrency QPS/RPS Testing and Optimization with SafeLine WAF
items.author: Maverick Steel
items.content:
In today’s digital landscape, web applications face an increasing number of requests per second (QPS) and connections per second (RPS). For organizations deploying self-hosted WAF solutions like , understanding and optimizing performance under high concurrency is crucial to ensure both security and reliability.

Understanding QPS and RPS

  • refers to the number of queries or HTTP requests processed by a system per second.
  • RPS (Requests Per Second) generally measures the same metric, though sometimes it may include TCP-level requests rather than full HTTP queries.

In a high-concurrency environment, distinguishing between QPS and RPS helps engineers evaluate whether performance bottlenecks arise at the network, transport, or application layer.

SafeLine WAF Performance Reference

SafeLine’s official testing shows that each CPU core can handle roughly in typical deployment scenarios. For example, a WAF node with could theoretically process under ideal conditions. However, real-world deployments often see lower throughput due to:

  • Network latency between load balancers (e.g., HAProxy) and the WAF
  • SSL/TLS handshake and encryption processing
  • Rule evaluation complexity

Testing High-Concurrency Scenarios

When performing QPS/RPS tests with SafeLine, consider the following:

  • Deploy SafeLine behind a load balancer.
  • Ensure WAF nodes have sufficient CPU and memory resources.
  • Use tools like ab (ApacheBench), wrk, or hey to simulate concurrent requests.
  • Gradually increase concurrency and track the QPS/RPS to identify bottlenecks.
  • Track CPU and memory usage on WAF nodes.
  • Observe latency and error rates from both the WAF and upstream applications.
  • Monitor network throughput to ensure no packet loss occurs under load.
  1. Leverage Multi-Core Processing
  • Enable SafeLine’s performance expansion mode on Pro licenses to utilize all CPU cores efficiently.

2. Tune Proxy and TCP Settings

  • Adjust proxy_connect_timeout and worker_connections in Nginx or HAProxy to handle high connection rates.
  • Use TCP keep-alive where appropriate to reduce handshake overhead.
  • Avoid overly broad or complex WAF rules that require extensive computation per request.
  • Utilize SafeLine’s selectively for high-risk endpoints.

4. Distributed Deployment

  • For massive traffic scenarios, deploy multiple WAF nodes in active–active or active–passive configurations.
  • Note: each node requires its own Pro license.
  • Integrate external traffic monitoring tools to validate real-world throughput and latency.
  • Compare observed QPS/RPS with theoretical expectations.

High-concurrency testing is essential for ensuring SafeLine WAF performs reliably under peak loads. By understanding QPS/RPS metrics, carefully monitoring system performance, and optimizing deployment parameters, organizations can maximize both and , ensuring seamless protection for all web applications.

Performance optimization in high-concurrency scenarios is a combination of adequate hardware, tuned system parameters, and well-planned WAF rule management. SafeLine provides flexible tools for administrators to balance security and scalability efficiently.

items.title: Feature Stores: Engineering Trust in Machine Learning
items.author: Okan Yenigün
items.content:
The Missing Link Between Data and Machine Learning

A feature store is a centralized repository for organizing, storing, and serving features.

It ensures that the same feature definitions and values used during model training are also used during real-time predictions, eliminating inconsistencies between training and serving environments.

A feature is a measurable property, attribute, or signal that represents some aspect of the phenomenon you want your model to learn from.

A feature is an input variable that your machine learning model uses to make predictions.

A model’s performance depends far more on the quality of its features than on the complexity of its architecture.

The math can be fixed, but the features define what the model can see.

Good features capture useful signals. Bad features introduce noise, bias, or leakage.

So a Feature Store is the systematic infrastructure that manages the lifecycle of these features — from creation and transformation to storage, serving, and governance.

Feature Engineering is the process of transforming raw data into meaningful features that make machine learning models more effective.

  • You start with raw data (logs, transactions, texts, clicks, etc.)
  • You apply domain knowledge and statistical transformations.
  • You create new, informative, and machine-readable features.

These engineered features are what the model actually learns from.

Feature engineering is where domain knowledge meets data science; you extract signals that make sense for your problem.

This means the features or data seen by the model during training are not identical to those it sees when making real-world predictions (serving time).

Even small mismatches can lead to big performance drops in production.

Let’s say you train a model to predict whether a customer will churn.

avg_purchase_last_30_days = total_spent_last_30_days / num_orders_last_30_days

You computed this using your data warehouse (batch mode) from yesterday’s data.

You want to make a real-time prediction when a user logs in. You recompute the same feature using live transactions from your application database, but:

  • The time window might be 29 days instead of 30,
  • Or you use a slightly different SQL query,
  • Or the data is delayed or missing.

This causes the model to receive slightly different input distributions at inference than what it saw at training, which yields accuracy drops, calibration breaks, and makes debugging difficult.

Collaborative Machine Learning

In every growing machine learning organization, one invisible bottleneck starts to appear as more models go into production, which is .

Teams work in isolation, reinventing the same transformations, recalculating the same aggregates, and debugging subtle inconsistencies between training and production.

That’s where the Feature Store (FS) steps in, the quiet backbone that keeps the entire machine learning ecosystem consistent, efficient, and collaborative.

You can imagine different teams, each generating features from their own domain expertise.

  • The marketing analytics team might calculate user engagement rates.
  • The payments team might generate average transaction amounts.
  • The customer success team could compute days since last support interactions.

In a mature ML organization, all these teams register their features in the Feature Store.

At the center sits the Feature Store. It’s more than just a database. It’s an interface for ML features, a shared layer that guarantees that:

  • The same logic used for generating features during training is also used during inference.
  • All teams share features, reducing duplication.
  • Every feature is versioned and documented, making experiments reproducible.

The “Consumers” no longer need to rebuild what others have already crafted, and can query the Feature Store.

The Churn Prediction Team or Fraud Detection team pulls features to use for training, validation, or runtime predictions.

A FS isn’t only a technical component — it’s a collaboration enabler. It encourages a mindset shift from “” to “”.

At its heart, as we have seen above, an FS lives between two worlds — data production and model consumption.

Every feature, no matter how advanced, must enter through ingestion and leave through consumption.

The ingestion side represents how features are created, transformed, and registered into the FS.

This process usually starts with raw data coming from:

  • Databases (e.g., PostgreSQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, …)
  • Event streams (e.g., Kafka, Pub/Sub, Kinesis, …)
  • Logs, APIs, or even batch CSV uploads

Once the data is collected, it goes through feature engineering:

  • Normalizations or statistical transformations

These features are then into the FS.

The consumption side is where the magic of machine learning actually happens. Once features are inside the store, they can be queried and used by different components:

  • Real-time inference services

Consumption can be batch or online. Either way, the FS guarantees the features are fresh, consistent, and reliable.

It takes place through two major pathways: ingestion and ingestion.

Batch Ingestion — Periodic Feature Creation

It begins with raw data, stored across various systems such as:

  • Databases like PostgreSQL or MongoDB

Data engineers and data scientists use tools like Spark, SQL, or Python scripts to process raw data in batches and extract features from it.

Example: Predicting delivery delays for a logistics company.

A logistics firm wants to train a model that predicts whether a package will arrive late. They need historical, aggregated data like:

  • avg_delivery_time_last_30_days
  • number_of_failed_deliveries_last_quarter
  • warehouse_utilization_rate

These don’t need to be updated in real time, so they’re generated in batches from historical data warehouses.

Batch processing using Spark, SQL, or Python aggregates data.

Once the features are computed, we associate them with an entity, the unique object they describe. An entity could be:

    This mapping is crucial because it tells the FS how to retrieve the correct feature when needed.

    Now, the FS takes over. It stores:

    • The entity associations (e.g., which product they belong to).

    Streaming Ingestion — Real-Time Feature Updates

      These aren’t stored as static tables like in batch systems; they are live event streams.

      • Streaming data is captured via Kafka, Kinesis, or Pub/Sub.
      • Stream processing (using Spark Streaming, Flink, or Beam) aggregates data.

      Streaming ingestion allows the FS to serve live, dynamic features that reflect the latest events.

      Once the ingestion pipelines (batch or streaming) have populated the FS with rich, up-to-date features, it’s time to consume them.

        Offline Consumption — Training & Evaluation

        ExamplePredicting customer churn for a telecom company.

        Data scientists extract historical features to train a model that predicts if a customer is likely to leave in the next month.

        • avg_monthly_call_duration
        • num_complaints_last_6_months
        1. Feature extraction: select a subset of features from the FS in bulk.
        2. Historical snapshot join: align feature values with label timestamps to avoid data leakage.
        3. Training & experimentation: use these features in notebooks (Jupyter) or pipelines (XGBoost, TensorFlow, PyTorch).

        Online Consumption — Real-Time Inference

        ExampleDynamic ride pricing in a mobility app.

        When a user opens the app and requests a ride, the system needs to instantly predict the  — factoring in:

        • Current traffic conditions
        • User’s past booking behavior
        • Weather and surge history

        The FS provides these features instantly (in milliseconds) via an online API.

        1. API request: model-serving layer receives a user_id and location.
        2. FS lookup: fetch the current feature vector for that user/location.
        3. Model inference: feed the feature vector into the pricing model.
        4. Response: return price estimate to the app within milliseconds.

        The online store (backed by Redis, DynamoDB, or Cassandra) ensures sub-10ms lookups for ultra-low-latency predictions.

                      ┌────────────────────────────┐              │         INGESTION          │
                      │ ───────────────────────── │
        Raw Data ───▶ │  Batch (Spark, SQL, Py)   │──┐
        Logs/Streams ▶│  Streaming (Kafka, Flink) │  │
                      └────────────┬──────────────┘  │                           ▼                 ▼
                             [ Feature Store ]────────────┐
                                   │                      │
                 ┌─────────────────┴──────────────────┐    │
                 ▼                                    ▼    ▼
        Offline Training & Eval                 Online Model Serving
         (Jupyter, TF, XGBoost)                 (APIs, Redis, TF Serving)

        What Actually Lives Inside a Feature Store

        A FS is not just a database — it’s a collection of specialized components that work together to make feature management consistent, performant, and production-ready.

        At its core, a feature store contains five layers:

        • On-Demand Transform [optional]

        Raw data is converted into usable, ML-ready features.

        It is optional, it’s common to handle transformations upstream (in Spark, Airflow, dbt, etc.) and let the FS focus purely on storing, serving.

        It is the heart of the feature store, where all computed feature values live.

        It is divided into two physical stores:

        • Offline Store holds historical feature data for model training. It is backed by systems like BigQuery, Snowflake, or S3/Parquet.
        • Online Store holds the most recent feature values for low-latency inference. Typically implemented with Redis, Cassandra, or DynamoDB.

        It delivers features to models and applications.

        The serving layer provides APIs and SDKs to:

        • Retrieve features for training (bulk extraction)
        • Fetch features for inference (real-time lookup)
        • Handle joins between entities

        This layer abstracts where the feature data comes from (offline vs online).

        It performs lightweight computations at request time during model serving.

        Not all features are precomputed. Some depend on live request data like:

        • Distance between the user location and the store location
        • Time difference between the current request and the last login

        The on-demand transform module combines these real-time inputs with precomputed features to form the final feature vector used by the model.

        The catalog and metadata manager for all features.

        The registry is like the “feature dictionary” — it doesn’t store data, but describes it. It contains:

        • Feature names and data types
        • Lineage (how the feature was derived)
        • Owners, versions, and freshness policies

        It enables a feature dictionary and reuse across teams, ensuring no one reinvents the same feature twice.

        It watches the health, freshness, and quality of features in real time.

        • Features are updated on schedule (no stale data)
        • Feature distributions don’t drift (detecting data drift)
        • Pipelines don’t break (failed ingestions, schema mismatches)

        For early-stage ML pipelines or single-team projects.

        • ✅ Registry (simple YAML or database)
        • ❌ No built-in transformation (handled elsewhere)

        Example: Using Feast with an offline store on BigQuery — transformations handled in Airflow, no streaming ingestion yet.

        2. Production-Grade Feature Store

        For mid-to-large-scale ML systems with real-time inference.

        • ✅ Offline & Online Storage
        • ✅ Serving API (real-time lookups)
        • ✅ Transformation Layer (batch + streaming)
        • ⚙️ On-Demand Transform (if model needs real-time computed features)
        • ⚙️ Monitoring (recommended for reliability)

        Example: Using Feast + Redis + Spark Streaming + Prometheus for a recommender system that needs low-latency user context updates.

        3. Enterprise / Advanced Feature Platform

        For large organizations or MLOps teams serving multiple business domains.

        • ✅ Full transformation layer (batch + stream)
        • ✅ Offline & Online Storage
        • ✅ Serving API with feature versioning
        • ✅ Registry with access control and lineage
        • ✅ On-Demand Transform (real-time computed context)
        • ✅ Operational Monitoring (freshness + drift dashboards)
        • ⚙️ Possibly vector store integration (for embeddings)

        Example: A financial institution using Tecton or Hopsworks, with governance, feature lineage, and live fraud detection.

        Feature Stores represent one of the most important bridges between data engineering and machine learning operations.

        • A feature is the atomic unit of ML.
        • Feature engineering transforms raw data into meaningful insights.
        • Feature stores make those features consistent, discoverable, and available in real time — whether for training or inference.
        • Batch and streaming ingestions ensure both historical and up-to-date data are captured.
        • Offline and online consumption guarantee models see the same version of features during training and inference, avoiding training-serving skew.
        • Registries, storage, and serving APIs form the foundation of a reliable feature infrastructure — while transformation and monitoring layers elevate it to production scale.

        At its heart, an FS is not just about storage — it’s about .

        It ensures every model, experiment, and team uses a single source of truth for features, accelerating innovation while preserving reliability.

items.title: Infrastructure as Code at Thomson Reuters with AWS CDK
items.author: Vu San Ha Huynh
items.content:
This post is cowritten by Danilo Tommasina and Lalit Kumar B from Thomson Reuters.

Large organizations often struggle with infrastructure management challenges including compliance issues, development bottlenecks and errors from inconsistent AWS resource creation across teams. Without standardized naming, tagging and policy enforcement, teams face repeated boilerplate code and difficulty accessing centrally-managed resources.

In this post, we will show you how Thomson Reuters developed an extension of the AWS Cloud Development Kit (CDK) to automate compliance, standardization and policy enforcement in Infrastructure as Code (IaC) scripts. We will explore the strategic reasoning behind this initiative, outline foundational design principles, and provide technical details on TR’s journey from concept to implementation. The solution accelerates and standardizes cloud infrastructure deployment and management through seamless integration between TR’s custom library and AWS CDK.

Thomson Reuters (TR) is one of the world’s leading information organizations for businesses and professionals. TR provides companies with the intelligence, technology, and human expertise they need to find trusted answers, enabling them to make better decisions more quickly. TR’s customers span the financial, risk, legal, tax, accounting, and media industries.

In a large organization that offers a variety of customer products, it is essential to manage numerous cloud resources effectively. This involves overseeing multiple AWS accounts, implementing access control or addressing financial tracking challenges. These tasks require the application of centrally defined standards and conventions, with additional requirements tailored to specific sub-organizations.

Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is an effective method for managing cloud resources. However, utilizing vanilla AWS CloudFormation for extensive and intricate infrastructure can pose challenges. It requires careful attention to naming conventions, tagging standards, security, and best practices for infrastructure deployments. Additionally, repeating infrastructure patterns across various services and products often leads to excessive use of copy-paste and dealing with boilerplate code. When projects require configurable and dynamic components – including conditionals, loops, repeatable patterns, and distribution to a large user base – delivering CloudFormation scripts can become quite cumbersome and prone to errors.

AWS CDK addresses these challenges by enabling IaC development in high-level programming languages like TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Java. AWS CDK Level 2 and 3 constructs simplify and reduce the amount of code to be written to manage complex infrastructure. It allows TR to create custom libraries that extend the vanilla AWS CDK with additional patterns and utilities. The extension libraries can also be distributed for multiple programming languages and package managers thanks to JSII. JSII enables TypeScript libraries to be automatically compiled and packaged for native consumption in each target language, allowing CDK libraries to be written once but used in many different programming environments.

Solution to optimize the process

In a medium to large company, different teams provide the fundamental infrastructure services (e.g. authentication and authorization, networking, security, financial tracking and optimization, base infrastructure provisioning, etc.) to enable use of the cloud for a large community of developers.

Figure 1 illustrates the conventional method involving teams producing documentation that outlines the usage of pre-deployed infrastructure. This includes naming and tagging standards, required security boundaries, default settings and other relevant guidelines. Subsequently, the implementation team reviews these documents and integrates the established rules into their tool chain consistently, often working in isolation. This results in inefficiencies, misinterpretation risks and maintenance challenges when specifications change.

Figure 1: The traditional approach

TR’s optimized approach replaces documentation with working code as shown in Figure 2.

Figure 2: The optimized approach

Infrastructure teams contribute their specifications into an extension library for AWS CDK, while the implementation teams can also contribute common patterns back into the central extension. The central extension library is released as polyglot packages allowing the implementation teams to pick the programming language that fits best to their knowledge.

With this approach, TR introduce a “shift-left” in the development and delivery lifecycle. Standards and best practices are introduced early, things are done right by default, and TR minimizes the risks of getting inappropriately configured resources to be deployed, which leads to a reduction in the number of governance and security incidents. Implementation delivery teams can share well architected patterns for re-use by other teams to improve overall effectiveness.

Key factors for the adoption of a framework are:

  • Simplicity, ease-of-use, self-service, and fast onboarding
  • Low maintenance effort and cost
  • Controlled roll-out, ability to quickly roll-back

With the above in mind, TR delivered a minimally invasive framework that can be enabled with a tiny set of custom code on top of vanilla AWS CDK code.

Using the TR-AWS CDK core library is straightforward – users simply import the package and adapt their entry point. From there, they can leverage standard AWS CDK code and documentation for most development tasks. There’s no need to learn custom construct classes or follow extensive specialized tutorials – vanilla AWS CDK knowledge is sufficient for most requirements. Additionally, developers can quickly incorporate open-source construct libraries through standard package managers. These third-party libraries integrate seamlessly with the TR implementation, automatically conforming to company standards without requiring additional configuration.

By managing distribution of the library following standard software packaging and release procedures TR enable consumers to adopt new capabilities in a controlled way, with the ability to roll-back to previous versions if something goes wrong during an update.

All this together allows TR to tick off the key factors listed above.

TR created a monorepo (monolithic repository) which is a version control strategy where multiple projects or packages are stored in a single repository. This approach offers several advantages over maintaining separate repositories for each package: unified versioning, simplified dependency management, consistent tooling, atomic changes across packages and improved collaboration.

This setup mirrors the configuration used by AWS CDK itself.

TR organized their monorepo following this structure:

  • : Defines dev dependencies and global scripts used by all packages
  • : contains the different modules
  • repo/packages/core/package.json: deps of core module and scripts for core module
  • : typescript code that composes the core module
  • repo/packages/core/lib/augmentation/*: module augmentations for AWS CDK core components
  • repo/packages/constructs-pattern-X: define multiple reusable and independent level 3 constructs
  • repo/packages/tr-cdk-lib/package.json: assembly module that defines scripts to assemble the final mono package that will be shared via a npm repository

This structure enables TR to maintain a collection of related, but distinct CDK constructs while making sure they work together seamlessly.

The modules are assembled and released into one single versioned package which simplifies the end-user’s consumption.

The core module: Foundation of TR AWS CDK library

The core module is the foundation of TR’s CDK extension library, it consists of several key components that work together to “TR-ify” AWS resources and offer simplified access to centrally managed infrastructure resources that are provided by TR’s AWS landing zone teams.

TR refers to “TR-ification”, as the process of dynamically adapting AWS CDK constructs to meet their standards and best practices. From a user perspective, the process happens in a minimally invasive way, for most of the time the user is coding with vanilla AWS CDK components, while having access to short-cuts to a variety of TR specific resources.

The core module serves several critical purposes:

  1. : makes sure the AWS resources follow TR naming conventions and tagging standards
  2. : abstracts away complex configurations required for TR compliance
  3. : provides seamless access to TR-managed resources like VPCs, security groups, and Route53 hosted zones
  4. : automatically applies custom security and financial optimization policies

The “TR-ification” process happens on every construct following a consistent order, for each construct it will:

  1. If applicable, set a name following a consistent pattern
  2. Apply custom initialization logic (e.g. set IAM permission boundary)
  3. Apply security and financial optimization defaults (if not set)
  4. Perform custom validations
  5. Verify security and financial optimization policies

TR uses a single root-level Aspect instead of multiple Aspects to avoid complex resource type checking and improve maintainability:

// This is the entrypoint that triggers the trification process on all CDK constructs
// we apply all TR specific transformations at this point
Aspects.of(this).add({
  visit: (node: IConstruct) => {
    node.getTRifier().trify();
  },
});

The careful readers at this point will scream: Wait a moment! node.getTRifier().trify() won’t compile!

Which is absolutely correct… unless you know a topic in TypeScript called module augmentation, in TR’s case, they augment the interface and class as follows:

/** Defines the set of functionality needed when trifying resources */
export interface ITRifier {
    trify(): void;
    readonly name: string | undefined;
    readonly nameFromTree: string;
}

declare module 'constructs/lib/construct' {
    interface IConstruct {
        /** Obtain the ITRifier responsible to add TR specific features to this CDK IConstruct */
        getTRifier(): ITRifier;
        
        trContext(): AppContext | StageContext | StackContext;
    }
    
    interface Construct extends IConstruct {
        /** Build the ITRifier responsible to add TR specific features to this CDK IConstruct */
        buildTRifier(): ITRifier;
    }
}

Then provide default implementations for the generic Construct:

Construct.prototype.getTRifier = function () {
    // Lazy getter, build the TRifier only when needed and cache it
    return ObjectUtils.lazyGetFrom(this, 'trifier', () => this.buildTRifier());
};

Construct.prototype.buildTRifier = function () {
    return new ConstructTRifier(this); // Default dummy implementation
};

Construct.prototype.trContext = function (): StackContext {
    return Stack.of(this).trContext() as StackContext;
};

Since AWS CDK constructs implement the interface, respectively extend the class automatically, the “TR-ification” process becomes available for many types of constructs. All you need to do now is inject your custom logic for all resources you need customization and make sure the module is loaded, e.g. in case of a Lambda function, it uses:

lambda.CfnFunction.prototype.buildTRifier = function () {
    return new CfnResourceTRifierLambda.CfnFunction(
        this,
        () => { // Accessor for retrieving the lambda function name
            return this.functionName;
        },
        (name: string) => { // Accessor for setting the lambda function name
                this.functionName = name;
        },
        () => {
            // Our own stuff to set defaults for financial optimizations
            const policyChecker = FinOps.Lambda.Defaults.apply(this);
            
            this.node.addValidation({
                validate: () => {
                    // Inject a custom validation logic to check compliance with financial policies
                    return policyChecker.addErrorIfNotCompliant(this);
                }
            });
        }
    );
};

TR targets L1 (Cfn) constructs like CfnFunction because the higher-level L2 and L3 constructs internally create L1 constructs during synthesis. This architectural decision makes sure TR-ification is applied universally, whether users write new lambda.Function() or new lambda.CfnFunction(), both will be TR-ified. This approach provides complete coverage with a single implementation point while remaining completely transparent to library users who can continue using their preferred abstraction level without awareness of this internal mechanism.

TR uses standardized naming to support IAM policy filtering and consistent resource management. In order to support a broad range of use-cases, TR defined the resource name pattern as follows:<segregationPrefix>[-appPrefix]-<resourceName>[-region]-<envSuffix> where the elements mean:

  • : A prefix used for grouping resources for a specific asset, it implies that a segregated administrative group is responsible for this resource, where applicable it is used for ARN based IAM resource filtering.
  • : Optional, a prefix used to map a resource to a specific application or service, this is shared across stacks within a CDK app.
  • : The name of a resource indicating its purpose.
  • : Optional, applied only to resources that are global but are part of a CDK stack that is bound to a specific region.
  • : A suffix used to segregate different deployment environments, e.g. development, continuous integration, quality assurance, production.

Traditional approaches require developers to manually construct these names, propagating prefixes and suffixes throughout their code:

new lambda.Function(stack, 'foo', {
    runtime: lambda.Runtime.NODEJS_LATEST,
    handler: 'index.handler',
    code: new lambda.InlineCode('bar'),
    functionName: `\${segregationPrefix}-\${appPrefix}-compute-stats-\${envSuffix}`,
});

With TR AWS CDK extension, the code is simplified to:

new lambda.Function(stack, 'MyFunction', {
  runtime: lambda.Runtime.NODEJS_LATEST,
  handler: 'index.handler',
  code: new lambda.InlineCode('foo'),
  functionName: 'compute-stats',
});

The describes what the function does without “noise”, TR AWS CDK will transparently generate and inject the name into the synthetized CloudFormation script, matching the specification. Note that is optional and TR-CDK will either TR-ify a provided name or automatically generate a valid one if the user omits it, making sure CloudFormation receives a properly formatted name.

Access to “Landing Zone” resources

TR’s central AWS Landing Zone team is responsible of inflating a set of standard resources (e.g. VPC, subnets, security groups, Route 53 zones, golden AMIs, etc.) into AWS accounts that are made available to application development teams.

Through module augmentation (shown earlier), the TR-ifier defines the function which provides access to a context-aware utility. When calling this function on a resource that resides within a , it will return an object that implements interface.

export interface StackContext extends StageContext {
  /** Get access to the TR IVpc */
  readonly vpc: IVpc;

  /** Provides access to standard security groups that are available in all TR accounts */
  readonly securityGroups: trparams.ISecurityGroupsResolver;

  /** Provides access to private and public hosted zones (with numeric digits) that are available in all TR accounts */
  readonly route53: trparams.IRoute53Resolver;

  /** Provides access to TR golden AMIs that are available in all TR accounts */
  readonly goldenAMI: TRGoldenAMI;
}

The attributes are accessors for the AWS Landing Zones resources listed above. With calls like the following examples, you have a simple way to obtain access to the standard VPC, subnets selections, route 53 private hosted zone, …

// Get the IVpc:
const trVpc: IVpc = stack.trContext().vpc;

// Get the private subnets as array
const privateSubnets: ISubnet[] = trVpc.privateSubnets;

// Get the private subnets as SubnetSelection
const privateSubSel: SubnetSelection = trVpc.selectSubnets({
    subnetType: SubnetType.PRIVATE_WITH_EGRESS,
});

// Get the private Route53 hosted zone
const privateHZ = stack.trContext().route53.privateHostedZone;

You might now wonder how TR resolves the resources and obtain objects implementing , , , …

Instead of using hard-coded resource attributes (e.g. Id, ARN, …) or complex lookups, TR uses CloudFormation’s ability to resolve Systems Manager parameters at execution time, as part of the AWS account initial inflation along with the resources, Systems Manager parameters are registered as well. The parameter names are the same across TR’s AWS accounts, the value contains e.g. the id of the matching AWS Landing Zone standard resource, e.g. /landing-zone/vpc/vpc-id, /landing-zone/vpc/subnets/private-1-id, /landing-zone/vpc/subnets/private-2-id, …

TR then defined custom , , … implementations and for each function they implemented dynamic resolution of resource attributes via Systems Manager parameters. With this approach, TR obtains portable code that runs on AWS accounts initialized via TR inflation process. There are no hard-coded resource identifiers, and there is no need for lookups via AWS SDK during synthesis.

As a user of the TR AWS CDK library, TR developers interact with an object implementing the interface and do not have to care about how to obtain e.g. the VPC-id and subnet ids. The same principle applies to Route53 hosted zones, Golden AMI ids, etc.

Application initialization

As mentioned previously, one key design principle is to minimize the custom code that a user of TR AWS CDK is required to use compared to using vanilla AWS CDK. This approach leverages existing AWS CDK and reduces the learning curve for developers.

This is how TR developers initialize an App with vanilla CDK, compared to how they initialize it with TR AWS CDK.

// Initialize a vanilla AWS CDK application
const app = new cdk.App()

// Initialize a TR CDK application
const app = TRCdk.newApp({
  segregationId: '123456',
  resourceOwner: 'team@example.com',
  namingProps: { prefix: 'myapp' },
  deploymentEnv: TRDeploymentEnv.DEV
});

From this point on, the developers can continue using vanilla AWS CDK code, the value returned by TRCdk.newApp(…) is an instance of an extension of CDK’s App class and is fully compatible with it. It, however, injects the TR-ification aspect, manages the tagging process, and initializes contextual information.

Here and there, e.g. when they need to pass the VPC into a construct, they will need to call TR AWS CDK code via the entry point that is exposed on CDK constructs through TypeScript’s module augmentation feature, but that’s it! 99% of the code is vanilla AWS CDK code.

The , , and attributes are used for multiple purposes like formatting resource names and tagging resources.

TR defines tagging standards, there are mandatory tags (e.g. for attribution to a specific product asset and for tracking resource ownership), and there are optional tags (e.g. for specifying resources that belong to different services within the same product asset).

The , the , and attributes are used to set mandatory tags using CDK’s built-in functionality for tagging. TR also defines a standardized set of optional tags that can be passed into the application context or set ad-hoc on individual constructs.

// Initialize a vanilla AWS CDK Application
const app = new cdk.App()

// Initialize a TR CDK application
const app = TRCdk.newApp({
  segregationId: '123456',
  resourceOwner: 'team@example.com',
  namingProps: { prefix: 'myapp' },
  deploymentEnv: TRDeploymentEnv.DEV
  optionalTRTags: {
    financialId: '123456789',
    projectName: 'my-project',
    serviceName: 'ServiceX',
    environmentName: 'Dev environment for ServiceX'
  }

This approach maintains consistency in the use of tag names and setting the values, it happens automatically behind the scenes and will be applied to the taggable constructs. No copy-pasting of tag definitions like in AWS CloudFormation, no issues dealing with CloudFormation’s inconsistent syntax for tag declarations, no forgetting of tagging resources.

In this post, we discussed how the monorepo approach to AWS CDK development, centered around the core module, has significantly improved the infrastructure management at Thomson Reuters. By providing well-architected L3 constructs, standardizing and simplifying AWS resource creation, they’ve reduced errors, enhanced governance, and accelerated development.

The core module’s ability to enforce policies, standardize naming and tagging, and provide access to TR-managed resources makes it an invaluable tool for teams working with AWS infrastructure at Thomson Reuters.
